sentences.text: Approximately 200, about 100, maybe 150, yes.
I can remember about 20 kids went to where I went to school.
I-- I was mostly engaged with the Gentile kids because they lived in my neighborhood.
The Jewish people that lived in our town were mostly merchants, and they commuted to Amsterdam.
And lived-- they don't live in our same neighborhood.
They lived in-- in big homes, maybe about 15, 20 minutes outside, like, suburbs of-- of Bussum.
They were nice.
I used to deliver parcels to them.
And they were very kind.
They always tipped me a nickel.
I always bring the flowers because they always ordered flowers for-- for a Friday.
That said, they were very good customers of my dad.
And they bought-- they were good flower buyers, so to speak.
I remember one village, you-- you had to go at least about 300, 400 yards before you get to their door.
But there was big dogs there.
I remember that one time-- you have to ring the bell, and the dogs are put away.
I go to deliver parcel there one day, and there are the dogs.
And I had to figure out how am I gonna get to the door?
I ring the bell, I ring the bell, nope.
I says, well, there's one way I gotta get down there.
And for my luck, the lane goes down.
It's like, you're going down towards the house.
So my little brain figures it out.
If I take a run out of this, I can get to the door without the dogs hitting  my feet.
And I'll put my feet up on the wheel, and they won't get to my feet.
And sure enough, I take a run at it.
I sit on the bike, put my feet up on the wheel, and [?
I went ?] by.
But they can't get to my feet.
By the time I was halfway, somebody got to the door and got the dogs away.
Meantime, I didn't get bit.
And that was my story of that.
And I used to-- that's why I say that I used to live outside, mostly outside the commute to Amsterdam.
There was a lot of people commuting to Amsterdam.
Because-- like you have here garages that are car parks, we had-- next door, there was a garage for bikes, bicycles.
They parked the bike.
And then they go to-- on the train, big place, yeah.
I used to help them out, too.
He used to give me a nickel help him putting the bikes away.
I've always found myself places to make money.
And maybe that was the businessman in me.
Yeah, I used to help him on-- and after, if my dad didn't have nothing to do for me, I'd go next door.
His name was Heny Boss, Heny Boss.
And I says, you got some work for me here?
Yeah, sure.
And I help him for about an hour-- the rush hours from-- maybe at night, from 5:00 to 6:00, most of them come home-- and handed people the bikes.
I kind of enjoyed it.
But that-- I like that.
I always tried to find something to do.
Nope.
Nope, the only thing that we belonged to was the soccer club.
Yeah, there was not too many organizations that'd be like you have here, but like the neighborhood or the [INAUDIBLE] or any of these organizations.
We didn't know much about them.
We didn't know much about any organizations, for that matter.
Maybe it sounds too simple, but that's-- we lived a very, very simple life.
And maybe that's nice, because here today, you can't live a simple life like this.
I mean, although I am.
I'm a very content person today.
We knew of Israel, yes.
We knew of Israel because our rabbi would remind us of it, but otherwise, no.
We didn't know about Canada.
We knew about New York.
We knew what New York was.
We knew it in the United States.
But Canada, to us, we learned in school was Eskimos and Indians and not the most-- I mean, they taught us.
The taught us-- in the higher grades, they taught you English, and they taught you French.
But otherwise, no, I can't remember anything that they were teaching us outside.
Yes, we learned from the West Indies because the West Indies, was-- they were owned by Holland.
And they were very rich from that.
I still remember them-- Bali, Lombok, Sumbawa, Flores.
And Flores was half-Portuguese.
See, I remember that.
Let's see, how many years ago we learned this?
We learned that in second grade.
And it comes to me after these many years.
I'm surprised.
Also they taught at school that you-- we had to learn how to swim.
Why did we have to learn how to swim?
Lots of water-- canals, ponds.
So first grade, it was mandatory.
They throw you in the water and they say, swim, oh, yeah.
Gymnastics was a-- was a must.
They taught you how to-- every day, it was part of the school day, gymnastics oh, yeah.
And just a-- those are the things they taught you.
But as far as outside, no, not much.
Well, the only thing I can remember-- again, I come back to Rabbi Vegehlen, who says, you know, you're a smart boy.
You should learn to be an architect, about landscaping.
Yes, how's that?
See, but Israel needs people like that.
So I went-- before the war, this is where-- I went to a special school to learn at it from there.
We'd go to-- at a high school, in a place called-- that-- it was in [INAUDIBLE], I remember.
And I was studying for my exams.
But the war-- The entrance exams?
Yeah, I was studying for exams to go to that school.
But the war did strange things, cut that off.
I would have liked to do that. that.
I took an interest in that because, you know, with my knowledge about the flowers and everything, I thought it would be a good thing.
And my dad thought it was a good thing, too.
Well, I saw some pictures.
The rabbi showed us some pictures from-- from Israel.
And he says, it's nice.
You'll be in between all your own people.
And he says, yeah, it'll a good idea.
And I worked towards that, but it never happened.
It never happened.
Jewish wedding-- the only Jewish wedding I can remember, after the war, my brother-in-law married my sister, Shelly.
And that's the only Jewish wedding I remember.
Because we didn't have old enough people.
The ones that were old enough, they were my aunties and uncles.
So-- and the children weren't old enough.
They were all my-- our age.
So there was not a-- there was nothing for us to go to.
There was-- it was-- nobody in the family was getting married.
They were too young.
So I-- I don't remember of any--
Nope, I do not remember.
Maybe something, but I-- nope, I do not remember any of that.
I remember a lot of things, but no.
Because maybe all these things didn't happen, you see?
Because again, we lived in a-- in a-- maybe if we would have-- would have lived in Amsterdam, maybe would be more knowledgeable about that.
But since we lived in a town-- again, I come back, a Gentile town-- we lived almost like them.
We lived-- we were Jewish, and we lived our own life.
But-- not that we become like Gentiles, but we lived in a Gentile town.
So therefore, it didn't come up-- it didn't come up in our lives.
Well, the reason we started noticing that there was something wrong, there was a lot of German Jews coming to work-- to live in Holland.
And we start to get knowledge of what was going on in Germany because of them.
There were hundreds, thousands of them in the '30s to come to live in-- in Holland.
In Bussum, oh, yes.
They-- they-- they settled wherever they had relatives, I guess, because there were German Jews in-- in-- in Holland from when I can remember.
And yes, in the '30s, there was a lot of German Jews coming to live there.
And then, slowly, towards '38, we started to hear about Mr.
Hitler.
We started to learn it was better to stay away from this man.
This man was no good to us.
And '39, sure, the-- the war came.
Yep.
And when they told us to go into hiding, the rabbi came one day to us.
And he says, there's a boat leaving for England.
So my mom says, no, I don't think so.
Why?
I don't know.
But she says, no, we're not going to go.
We're not going to do that.
And sure enough, that boat got torpedoed.
And they don't see-- everybody drowned from that-- from that boat.
And that was a good thing that we didn't go.
[LAUGHTER]
So she must have some foresight.
But in-- as a kid, like I said, in 1939-- it was 1940 that they walked into Holland.
And the first thing we noticed, I was outside, watching the train come in.
It was-- and there was the Jewish soldiers.
Not the Jewish soldiers-- I beg your pardon; mind you, it might have been Jewish soldiers-- but a train full of wounded soldiers.
And then we knew we were at war.
And that's when-- that was the-- the time that my mother says, well, we better get-- get out of here, because their coming here, it's no good to us.
And when-- we found out from Rabbi Vegelahn at that time-- but we didn't want to go because they didn't bomb over us yet, you see?
But when they-- when they started putting stars, that's-- we were branded.
OK?
Tell me about that, how you became aware of that.
How we became aware of that?
We couldn't go to school.
They took the bags away from us.
They took the radio away from us.
Germans, Germany.
Oh, yeah, they came.
They took everything away.
They took that away.
We couldn't go to school.
They took away from us.
And-- and then we know there's-- there was something wrong, that we definitely knew there was something wrong.
Rabbi Vegelahn.
Let's see, they call it the-- the-- the Jewish-- how would you explain this to you?-- the Jewish-- in Dutch, it's [DUTCH].
It's-- it's-- it's a committee that looks after whatever the Germans tell you to-- them to do.
And they came to bring them.
And we had to sew them on all our clothes.
That's right, that's right.
There you go.
And from that day on, we were branded.
How do I know?
I go on-- on the day that I usually play soccer, I go to the soccer game to go play my game.
And there-- there's a policeman standing there.
I know him very well because his son plays on the same team with me.
He says, [INAUDIBLE], you can't go in.
I says, how is that?
He said, read the sign.
And the sign says, 'No Jews Allowed.' And I tell you, I was flabbergasted.
I said, why?
Why?
What did I do wrong?
I said, I didn't do anything wrong.
Did I steal anything?
Did I kill anybody?
He says, well, the Germans don't want you to play soccer.
He says, they should all go to hell.
And I cried bitterly.
I ran home.
I swear, as of today, I'll never forget.
They won't let me-- a kid.
How can you tell a kid-- and at that time, I was 12-- you can't play soccer?
My favorite game?
And I came home.
And I says-- and my father was there.
He says, what's the matter?
Why don't you play?
[INAUDIBLE] won't let me.
He said, what do you mean he won't let you?
I said, there's a sign there-- 'Jews Not Allowed.' My father got so mad, he took a-- a vase, he slammed it in the ground.
He says, what the heck is coming off here?
I'll never forget that.
I'll never forget that.
And that's how it started.
I forget-- the Germans, they didn't-- they didn't bother us, really, at that particular moment.
But those were little things.
You weren't-- they come on the sidewalk, get off the sidewalk!
You had to get off the sidewalk.
You don't get off the sidewalk, you get-- you get knocked down.
They did it to my dad.
He walked, and he wouldn't get off.
And they threw him off the sidewalk.
And it's lucky-- the fellow that was from the bicycle garage, he held my dad back.
My dad was going at him.
And a good thing he held him back, otherwise he wouldn't be here today.
Oh, he wanted to, oh, yeah.
But that-- in those days, you didn't know what was going to happen to you.
And like I said, as a kid, sometimes you do not understand.
You definitely don't understand.
Because circus is in town, I can't go in.
I can see that-- that they set it up.
And in those days, we had a curfew. 8 o'clock, all Jews off the street.
So I forgot my time.
I'm standing there because you're intrigued.
I'm 12 years old.
I love the circus.
And they're building this.
And the next thing I know, I'm surrounded by six of these Germans.
They're standing around me.
And I didn't notice it until I look at the wall-- at the clock, because it was near the station.
It's a minute to 8:00.
They won't let me out.
I panicked.
I panicked.
I said, how am I am going to get out of this thing?
And they're laughing.
The 'kleine Yidden,' you know, they talk in German.
I understand some of it.
I says, I have to be home by 8:00.
Now, will you please let me get out of here?-- in my broken German because we did know a little bit of German.
It took a couple of minutes.
They still wouldn't let me.
And I don't where I had the strength, I just went like this, boom!
I flew right through his legs.
I ran home.
And I got home in time.
But those were the things that sit here.
You always say, what hurts you?
You can't always say what's hurt you.
You can say, here, I have a cut from a knife.
You can show that.
But what is in your heart, you can't always tell people.
Because even if you tell people, they don't understand.
How can you-- how-- a kid that wants to go to a circus, I, mean, you know, and they take that away from you?
And those are things that stand by.
They'll never forget-- you'll never forget.
You forget, you put it behind you, somewhere back there it sits.
And I'm going to live till 95.
But I'll still remember, you know what I'm saying?
So those were the bad days.
And we knew what was coming.
And Vegelahn says, you better get out of here because-- they-- they-- they first started in Amsterdam.
And when they started in Amsterdam-- when they finished in Amsterdam, they came here.
So the first people that we found was people with the name of Baedeker-- there you go, Baedeker, nice people.
It was a young man with his mother, old lady, very nice old lady.
And we're not all-- got together in the same home.
Because they took the-- a doctor by the name of Dr.
Martin, beautiful man.
He took one of my brothers, Juda-- and he took him to the hospital and hid him there.
And there was my brother, Alan.
No, no, they just put him in-- in a room as a patient.
And then they took my brother, Alan-- a doctor by-- by the name of [?
Boen, ?] Dr.
Boen-- ?] and he took him also to where he was a doctor.
And he had him there, gave him a room.
But people are funny, even in Holland.
The Dutch people were good people.
But you had the people that-- didn't have to say-- they-- I don't want you call them.
We used to call them [? 'Christlings,' ?] the Black Shirts, the Socialists.
They didn't like the Jews.
They went with Hitler, OK?
And I'm getting ahead of myself.
But that's where we stayed for a while.
And from there, I remember there was a friend.
We couldn't stay there for the simple reason that one-- that's why I said that people are bad.
Some people are bad.
And there were some people in Holland.
Although they were Christian-- but they were bad people.
They snitched on my brother who was in the hospital.
The Gestapo picked him up, took him to Amsterdam.
Alan, yeah.
The Gestapo picked him up and took him to Holland.
We were already in hiding.
So my brother-- my mother-- I don't know how she had the guts.
At night, she took us about, went to Amsterdam.
And for some odd reason, they told-- my brother told the people that were there, I have no family.
They wanted to know where we are.
And they asked him, where is your-- he says, I'm an orphan.
He kept on saying he's an orphan.
Protect our-- to protect us.
So when my mother got there-- and the lady says to-- to him, your mother is downstairs.
He says, I have no mother, because he thought they were tricking him.
So the-- the nurse-- the people-- the person says, not to worry, it is your mother.
So when he came down, he saw my mother.
Then everything was all right.
And she brought him back.
And as we said, we are not-- we are not religious.
But I believe somebody looks over you.
I'm a fatalist.
If something has to happen to you, it happens.
And if it's good, you say, thank you.
And when it's bad, you learn to live with it.
And that's the way I-- that the way I think about that.
She brought them back, yeah.
My uncle, Louis, was still there.
And two sisters of my father, she brought them back at the same time.
And we found a spot for them.
But by him being picked up from the Gestapo, we couldn't stay-- just to say something leaked out, we couldn't stay where we were.
We thought, we thought.
And my sister came up with the name of Makkinje, Annie Makkinje.
She used to go to school with her-- very nice people, simple people, and they needed the money.
It's not because they were-- I mean, they wanted to hide us.
I mean, they wanted to-- they took us in.
But for them, the money was important.
So that's why we wound up at the Makkinjes'.
And that's the room.
That's how the room started.
Gosh, the exact time?
The exact date?
About-- I would-- I would say that was in the-- in the '40s because from the '40s to the-- it was-- the total that we were in hiding was about three years.
But we stayed most of that time in that room.
The room?
The room was a room like we're sitting in here, maybe a little bit bigger.
Oh, god, the dimension-- it was maybe about 12' by 15', 16'.
That's about a--
It was, yeah, it was a bedroom.
It was Annie's bedroom.
So my sister was sleeping on the cot, my father and mother in the bed, and we other ones slept on the floor.
That's how we managed.
Eight, eight, but we managed.
We slept on the floor.
You get-- you get used-- that's why maybe I have a bad back.
And the end of the first months, we had to get accustomed to it, you know what I'm saying?
Because you're always together.
But we were not children that fight.
We never argued, even when we were at home.
We never argued.
People always asked, you have six children?
My aunties had two.
And one had one.
She says, I have more trouble with my one than you have with your six.
How come?
She says, my kids are good.
I'm blessed with good children.
They're well-behaved.
And that's what we were.
We were well-behaved together.
We were brought up properly.
So we didn't fight with each other.
And if we have an argument, we got it out.
We talked it out.
And then, that's how it was.
We knew we were going to be in there, so you couldn't fight.
You couldn't make noise.
Should-- You told me that your strict upbringing, like, about being quiet, helped you when you were in hiding.
That's true.
That's true, that's definitely true.
Because I don't think any other children would have been able to behave like we behaved for all these years that we were in hiding.
We found things to do.
Well, my brother, Joe, used to read a lot.
My sister used to help my mother clean the-- the house-- the room, so to speak.
I used to read-- read a lot of cowboy stories.
Yeah, that's a good idea-- a good thing.
Annie, Annie used to go to the library.
And she used to get the books.
She used to get a lot of books, I believe.
Oh, yeah, a lot of books.
We used to-- I mean, that's the way we kept busy till I-- I-- I didn't have too much patience to read the books.
I found myself a spot.
There was a staircase.
And there was a window.
And I put a little hole so I could peek through so they wouldn't see where it was.
And I kept myself busy, watching people on the-- on the street.
They kept me busy, daydreaming.
That's-- maybe one day, you'll get out there again.
And as years went along, it was painful.
Well, I used to watch the people get up.
He's the guy-- the people from the city cleaned the street.
I used to watch the people go to school, the kids go to school.
I watched the-- the-- the farmer bring his horses in, different things, you know, that people do, daily-- daily work, the people's daily work.
And I also watched who was coming around the corner, you know.
Because there-- there were raids.
They used to pick up-- not the Jewish bad guy, the Gentile to go to work in Germany, OK?
That's why I says, well-- my dad, he was very smart guy.
What if they come here, I said?
He says, well, we'll [? build ?] a double room underneath the staircase.
It was like, it came down.
And there was a room underneath the staircase.
So he-- he made a door that you could open, but you couldn't see.
Once it was closed-- he was a very clever man-- you couldn't see.
And we put a wall.
On the wall, we put extra insulation, so if they knocked the door, then it-- then it wasn't-- wasn't-- it didn't sound hollow.
What did he put for insulation?
What did he put?
Blankets, yeah, he put blankets.
That's right, he put blankets.
And when you knocked, it was-- it was right.
We tried it out.
And it was holding exactly eight people.
For some reason, it holds eight people.
So we-- you know, we used to daily-- every other day, we used to run in.
You know.
We held the-- we trained to go in there.
Drills.
A simple-- a simple word like 'drills' I can't remember.
Yeah, we had drills.
I would-- well, I would sit in the-- on the chair, downstairs, watching out.
And I would come up, 'scram!' And we would all scram into the-- into the room.
And Mrs.
Makkinje would lock the door, because you had to lock it from the outside.
You know.
Oh, there was a few things there, naturally.
So we had to clean the room fast, like, so there-- there-- that it didn't look like somebody was living there.
Mind you, she made up a story.
She has refugees from Rotterdam because Rotterdam was bombed very badly.
Well, the raid-- I found out that there was a raid through-- a person that was walking outside said, the Germans are coming.
I don't know what they want.
But they're gonna come.
And sure enough, I see-- because I could see for the-- to the end of the street-- I see a big truck loaded with Germans.
I said, that must be it.
I ran upstairs.
I says, scram!
And we went everything orderly.
We didn't panic.
For some reason, we never panicked.
For some reason, we never panicked.
Because we had that feeling-- if something has to happen, it's going to happen.
And we're-- we're at ease with that.
Yeah, we get into the closet.
And sure enough, [? you got ?] Germans-- Mrs.
Makkinje was home.
And that's when we heard her say, what is this room?
Oh, it's for refugees.
And they knocked on the wall.
And they knocked on the wall.
And see, we're not [? found. ?] But somebody upstairs looked after us.
We were firm believers that somebody looked after us.
Yeah.
Yeah, they couldn't hear because it was not hollow.
It was not hollow.
See, if it would have been hollow, they would have figured, well, maybe there's something inside there.
They would ask, well, what is in there?
See, that's what happened with Anne Frank.
They found them.
They didn't have a hiding place.
That's way they got-- that's why they were arrested.
I saw that movie three times.
I saw that movie three times.
See, that was the difference in our story.
No, no thoughts at all, no thoughts at all.
That's why I say, we were at ease with-- if something has to happen, it's going to happen.
So we were never scared, never.
It never came into our mind to be scared.
As of today, I'm not scared of anything.
Bad stomach, nervous stomach, I'm left with that.
But no, we were never, ever, ever scared.
And I come to stories-- I'll tell you later-- because I was never scared for the simple reason you can't sidetrack-- step something if it's coming to you.
You can-- and we-- that's why I said-- and we have this from my mother.
My mother was never scared.
Don't ever-- even when we were children, she said, don't ever be scared of anything.
Tell the truth.
Behave like a good kid.
And the truth you can repeat all the time.
A lie you can't, a lie you can never say twice because it's always a different story.
The story I'm telling you now, I can repeat 10 times, every time till I'm 95.
I'm going to live till I'm 95, at least I want to.
Good.
